steps.text: Evaluate the acceptability of multi-metals continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS) at the time of or soon after installation.
Calibrate, maintain, and operate the CEMS.

preconditions.text: Ensure the CEMS is capable of measuring the total concentrations of two or more of the target metals (Sb, As, Ba, Be, Cd, Cr, Pb, Hg, Ag, Tl, Mn, Co, Ni, Se) in both their vapor and solid forms.
Ensure the diluent (O2) monitor meets its own performance specifications (40 CFR part 60, Appendix B, Performance Specification 3).

constraints.text: If a CEMS does not measure a particular metal or fails to meet the performance specifications for a particular metal, then the CEMS may not be used to determine emission compliance with the applicable regulation for that metal.

steps.text: Calibrate, maintain, and operate the CEMS.
Conduct CEMS performance evaluations.
Conduct reference method tests and calibration drift tests to determine conformance of the CEMS with the specification.
Document the span value.

preconditions.text: The Administrator requires, under Section 114 of the Act, the operator to conduct CEMS performance evaluations at other times besides the initial test.

constraints.text: The span value shall be defined as twenty times the applicable emission limit for each metal.

steps.text: Install the CEMS at an accessible location downstream of all pollution control equipment where the metals concentrations measurements are representative.
Select representative measurement points or paths for monitoring.
Select traverse points to determine the representativeness of the measurement location according to 40 CFR part 60, Appendix A, Method 1, Section 2.2 and 2.3.
Relocate the CEMS as required by the Administrator.

preconditions.text: If the cause of failure to meet the RA test is determined to be the measurement location and a satisfactory correction technique cannot be established

constraints.text: The measurement location should be at least eight equivalent diameters downstream of the nearest control device, point of pollutant generation, bend, or other flow disturbance, and at least two equivalent diameters upstream from the effluent exhaust.
For Point CEMS, the measurement point should be no less than 1.0 meter from the stack or duct wall, or within or centrally located over the centroidal area of the stack or duct cross section.

For Path CEMS, the effective measurement path should be totally within the inner area bounded by a line 1.0 meter from the stack or duct wall, or have at least 70 percent of the path within the inner 50 percent of the stack or duct cross sectional area, or be centrally located over any part of the centroidal area.

steps.text: Calculate the equivalent duct diameter as per 40 CFR part 60, Appendix A, Method 1, Section 2.1.
Select traverse measurement point locations according to 40 CFR part 60, Appendix A, Method 1, Sections 2.2 and 2.3.
Displace the RM traverse line up to 30 cm (or 5 percent of the equivalent diameter of the cross section, whichever is less) from the centroidal area.

preconditions.text: The RM traverse line interferes with or is interfered by the CEMS measurements

constraints.text: The RM measurement location must be at least eight equivalent diameters downstream of the nearest control device, point of pollutant generation, bend, or other flow disturbance, and at least two equivalent diameters upstream from the effluent exhaust.
The RM and CEMS locations should be as close as possible without causing interference with one another.

The CEMS data recorder response range must include zero and a high level value equal to the span value, or be capable of providing multiple outputs / automatically changing the high level value so the measured value does not exceed 95 percent of the high level value.

The RA of the CEMS must be no greater than 20 percent of the mean value of the RM test data in terms of units of the emission standard for each metal, or 10 percent of the applicable standard, whichever is greater.
The CEMS design must allow the determination of calibration drift.

steps.text: Perform calibration at a point equal to 80 to 120 percent of the applicable emission standard for each metal.
Sample the stack effluent continuously.
Conform averaging time, number of measurements in an average, and the averaging procedure for reporting and determining compliance with the applicable emission regulation.
Receive a waiver from the response time requirement from the Agency.
Ensure the sampling time is no longer than one third of the averaging period for the applicable standard.
Ensure the delay between the end of the sampling period and reporting of the sample analysis is no greater than one [unit].

preconditions.text: If zero drift determination at zero level is not possible or practicable, allow the zero drift determination to be made at a low level value (zero to 20 percent of the emission limit value).
If no CEM is available which can meet the response time specification at the time of purchase of the CEMS.

constraints.text: The CEMS calibration may not drift or deviate from the reference value (RV) of the calibration standard used for each metal by more than 5 percent of the emission standard for each metal.
The CEMS zero point for each metal shall not drift by more than 5 percent of the emission standard for that metal.
The response time for the CEMS must not exceed 2 minutes to achieve 95 percent of the final stable value.

steps.text: Install the CEMS and prepare the RM test site according to the specifications in Section 3.
Prepare the CEMS for operation according to the manufacturer’s written instructions.
Determine the magnitude of the calibration drift (CD) and zero drift (ZD) once each day (at 24-hour intervals) for 7 consecutive days according to the procedure given in Section 6.
Conduct a RA test according to the procedure given in Section 7.

preconditions.text: The affected facility is operating at more than 50 percent of normal load, or as specified in an applicable subpart.
No unscheduled maintenance, repair, or manual adjustment of the CEMS took place during the preceding 24-hour period.

constraints.text: The sampling time must be no longer than one third of the averaging period for the applicable standard.
The delay between the end of the sampling period and reporting of the sample analysis shall be no greater than one hour.
The pause in sampling when the sample collection media are changed should be no greater than five percent of the averaging period or five minutes, whichever is less.
None of the CD’s or ZD’s may exceed the specification.

steps.text: Conduct the CD test immediately before periodic automatic or manual adjustments, or conduct it in such a way that the CD and ZD can be determined.
Conduct the CD and ZD tests at the points specified in Sections 4.3 and 4.4.
Record the CEMS response and calculate the CD.
Calculate the ZD.
Conduct the RM measurements in such a way that they will yield results representative of the emissions from the source and can be correlated to the CEMS data.

preconditions.text: If periodic automatic or manual adjustments are made to the CEMS zero and calibration settings

constraints.text: Conduct the diluent (if applicable), moisture (if needed), and pollutant measurements simultaneously, or within a 30- to 60-minute period.
The concentration of each metal must be detectable by both the CEMS and the RM.
Determine the RA at three levels (0 to 20, 40 to 60, and 80 to 120 percent of the emission limit) for one of the metals which will be monitored, or for iron.

steps.text: Determine RA at three levels (0 to 20, 40 to 60, and 80 to 120 percent of the emission limit) for one of the metals which will be monitored, or for iron.
Note the beginning and end of each RM test period of each run (including the exact time of day) in the CEMS data log.
Correlate the CEMS and RM test data as to the time and duration by first determining from the CEMS final output the integrated average pollutant concentration for each RM test period.
Confirm that the pair of results are on a consistent moisture, temperature, and diluent concentration basis, considering system response time if important.
Compare each integrated CEMS value against the corresponding average RM value.
Obtain a minimum of three pairs of CEMS and RM measurements for each metal required and at each level required.

preconditions.text: If iron is chosen, the three levels should be chosen to correspond to those for one of the metals that will be monitored using known sensitivities (documented by the manufacturer) or the CEMS to both metals.

If more than nine pairs of measurements are obtained, then up to three pairs of measurements may be rejected so long as the total number of measurement pairs used to determine the RA is greater than or equal to nine.

constraints.text: All data, including the rejected data, must be reported.

steps.text: Summarize in tabular form the results of the CD tests and the RA tests or alternate RA procedure as appropriate.
Include all data sheets, calculations, and records of CEMS response necessary to substantiate that the performance of the CEMS met the performance specifications.
Report the CEMS measurements to the agency in units of µg/m on a dry basis, corrected to 20°C or 7 percent O.

preconditions.text: Check with the appropriate regional office, or State, or local agency for additional requirements.
